edges.description: Evasion of cuproptosis through PRR15 silencing and FDX1 repression allows transformed renal epithelial cells to escape copper-dependent cell death, increasing tumor invasiveness and metastatic progression.

nodes.description: Cuproptosis is a copper-dependent form of regulated cell death that acts as a tumor suppressor in RCC. Ferredoxin 1 (FDX1) serves as a core regulator of cuproptosis and its associated mitochondrial dysfunction. PRR15 (Proline-rich 15) promotes cuproptosis through a regulatory axis, but during RCC development, PRR15 is silenced. This silencing activates NF-κB signaling, which represses FDX1 expression, blocking cuproptosis and promoting tumor invasiveness and metastasis. The evasion of cuproptosis contributes to RCC progression by allowing tumor cells to escape this cell-death pathway while maintaining enhanced lipid metabolism and altered oxidative stress regulation in the tumor microenvironment.

evidence.snippet: PRR15 promotes cuproptosis and mitochondrial damage in RCC cells and inhibits tumor proliferation and metastasis, as demonstrated in vivo and in vitro. When RCC develops, PRR15 silencing activates the nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-κB) signaling pathway, which inhibits FDX1 expression, ultimately blocking the cuproptosis process and increasing tumor invasiveness.

nodes.description: Malignant transformation of renal epithelial cells gives rise to renal cell carcinoma with diverse molecular subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Systemic treatment of renal cell cancer: A comprehensive review.
evidence.snippet: The most common form of kidney cancer arises from renal epithelium, named renal cell carcinoma (RCC).
